name: Hinojosa del Duque from Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo — circular by Villanueva del Duque
name_original: Hinojosa del Duque desde Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo — circular por Villanueva del Duque
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo traverse diverse landscapes, from the scenic Sierra Boyera Reservoir to the broader Andalusian countryside. The region features varied terrain, including both paved surfaces and segments that may be unpaved, offering a mix of challenges. Cyclists can experience the Guadiato Valley and surrounding mountain ranges, providing ascents and descents. The area's topography includes cultivated fields and olive groves, characteristic of the region. Best road cycling routes around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo The most popular road cycling route is Circular Route of Peñarroya, a 44.4 miles (71.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 11 minutes to complete. This route offers a moderate challenge through the local landscape. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Hinojosa del Duque from Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo — circular by Villanueva del Duque, a moderate 52.8 miles (85.1 km) path. This route provides extensive views of the surrounding countryside and takes approximately 3 hours 38 minutes. Local road cyclists also love the Belmez, Espiel, Villanueva del Rey — circular desde Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo, a 49.7 miles (80.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 3 hours 51 minutes. Road cycling around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo is defined by the Guadiato Valley, the Sierra Boyera Reservoir, and the Andalusian countryside with its cultivated fields. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo?
A: The region offers diverse terrain, from routes circling the scenic Sierra Boyera Reservoir to the broader Andalusian countryside. You'll find a mix of mostly paved surfaces suitable for various skill levels, alongside segments that may be unpaved and more challenging. Expect ascents and descents as you navigate the Guadiato Valley and surrounding mountain ranges, often passing through cultivated fields and olive groves.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo?
A: Yes, there are routes suitable for beginners. While many routes are moderate, komoot lists 1 easy road cycling route in the area. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, making them ideal for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What are the typical distances and durations for road cycling routes in this area?
A: Road cycling routes around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo vary in length and duration. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016711880/circular-route-of-penarroya" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Peñarroya&lt;/a&gt; is about 44.4 miles (71.5 km) and takes around 3 hours 11 minutes. Longer routes, like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123131" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villaralto – Belmez Castle loop from Belmez&lt;/a&gt;, can extend to over 56 miles (91 km) and take closer to 4 hours.

Q: Can I find challenging road cycling routes with significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1335246248/belmez-espiel-villanueva-del-rey-circular-desde-penarroja-pueblonuevo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belmez, Espiel, Villanueva del Rey — circular desde Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain, reaching over 1000 meters (3280 feet) of ascent over 49.7 miles (80 km). This route is classified as difficult and provides a demanding experience through varied terrain.

Q: What historical or cultural points of interest can I see while cycling?
A: The region is rich in industrial heritage. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593129" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Greenway of Mining&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique cycling experience past historical sites such as the former Metallurgical Mining Society's management building, the old Mining Hospital, and the French school. You can also spot the chimneys of the old industrial zone and visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5360266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belmez Castle&lt;/a&gt; on some routes.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints or natural features along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes offer picturesque views. The Sierra Boyera Reservoir is a prominent natural feature, providing scenic backdrops for rides. While the Peñón de Peñarroya is primarily a hiking attraction, cyclists can enjoy panoramic views of the Guadiato Valley and surrounding mountain ranges from various points along the routes.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo?
A: The road cycling routes in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the scenic beauty of the Guadiato Valley, and the mix of moderate and challenging options available.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. Popular examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016711880/circular-route-of-penarroya" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Peñarroya&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016807861/hinojosa-del-duque-from-penarroja-pueblonuevo-circular-by-villanueva-del-duque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hinojosa del Duque from Peñarroja-Pueblonuevo — circular by Villanueva del Duque&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo?
A: The broader Andalusian region, including Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable, while winters are typically mild but can have cooler days.

Q: Are there any specific attractions or landmarks related to the region's mining history?
A: Beyond the Greenway of Mining, you can explore the area's rich mining past by visiting sites like the Museo Geológico-Minero and the Barrio Francés (French Quarter) in Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo. These offer insights into the town's significant industrial heritage and can be incorporated into your cycling excursions.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo area?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around Peñarroya-Pueblonuevo on komoot, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. These include 1 easy route, 14 moderate routes, and 1 difficult route.
tips: Small and quiet town that until 1833 belonged to Extremadura. To get to know its nooks and crannies, the town council proposes to walk the Ruta del Agua (1 km) to discover a typical urban landscape of the Valley of Los Pedroches. The route begins at the Cruz de la Fuente Vieja, to continue through the Pozo de la Fuente Vieja, Pozo del Morconcillo, Fuente Plaza Doña María Josefa Fernández and ends at the Pozo del Verdinal, where there is a 19th century waterwheel. The route ends at the Peñalá corner.
Hinojosa del Duque is a municipality in the province of Córdoba in the region of Valle de los Pedroches. Population known for Holy Week and its processions.

Town that was born with the union of two independent municipalities: Peñarroya and Pueblonuevo del Terrible, which grew based on the mining activity of the region in the 19th century. The industrial boom was driven mainly by the Peñarroya Mining and Metallurgical Society (SMMP). The old SMMP buildings were converted after their decline in the mid-20th century into public buildings, such as a health center or geriatric hospital.
